Notes

Estimates here may not match other sources due to enrollment categorization differences and differences in measures of Medicaid spending. The spending data used in these estimates are from Medicaid claims which reflect spending at the time health care services are used or premium payments are made, whereas other measures of spending may reflect the timing of state payments to providers. Additionally, estimates here do not include prescription drug rebates and most supplemental payments to providers, which other Medicaid spending sources may include.  A small number of Medicaid enrollees whose eligibility group is unknown are not broken out into a separate column but are included in state and national totals. States with "Unusable" data quality according to the CMS DQ Atlas indicators "Medicaid-Only Enrollment", "Eligibility Group Code", "Age" and "Medicaid Beneficiary Expenditures" are not included in these tables and are not included in national totals. For more information on data quality issues that may lead to unusual spending or enrollment, see CMS DQ Atlas. Caution is recommended when comparing data between years since different states may be included in each year due to data quality.
Sources
KFF analysis of the T-MSIS Research Identifiable Files, 2023 (Preliminary)

*N/A:* Not Applicable. These states had not adopted the ACA Medicaid expansion as of the reporting year. *NSD:* Not Significant Data. Due to data security measures, the values of cells with fewer than 50 enrollees are not reported but are included in state and national totals. *DQ:* Data Quality. Data categorized as "Unusable" based on the DQ Atlas. These values are not included in the national totals, unless otherwise specified in a footnote. *Enrollees:* Individuals who are enrolled in Medicaid at any time during the calendar year. *Seniors:* Enrollees age 65 and older. *People with Disabilities:* Enrollees under age 65 who are reported as eligible due to a disability. *Adults:* Enrollees ages 19 to 64 who are not eligible due to a disability or newly eligible for Medicaid by the ACA Medicaid expansion. *Children:* Enrollees ages 18 and younger who are not eligible due to a disability. *ACA Expansion Adults:* Enrollees ages 19 to 64 who were made newly eligible for Medicaid by the ACA Medicaid expansion.

Definitions

*N/A:* Not Applicable. These states had not adopted the ACA Medicaid expansion as of the reporting year. *NSD:* Not Significant Data. Due to data security measures, the values of cells with fewer than 50 enrollees are not reported but are included in state and national totals. *DQ:* Data Quality. Data categorized as "Unusable" based on the DQ Atlas. These values are not included in the national totals, unless otherwise specified in a footnote. *Enrollees:* Individuals who are enrolled in Medicaid at any time during the calendar year. *Seniors:* Enrollees age 65 and older. *People with Disabilities:* Enrollees under age 65 who are reported as eligible due to a disability. *Adults:* Enrollees ages 19 to 64 who are not eligible due to a disability or newly eligible for Medicaid by the ACA Medicaid expansion. *Children:* Enrollees ages 18 and younger who are not eligible due to a disability. *ACA Expansion Adults:* Enrollees ages 19 to 64 who were made newly eligible for Medicaid by the ACA Medicaid expansion.

Notes

Estimates here may not match other sources due to enrollment categorization differences and differences in measures of Medicaid spending. The spending data used in these estimates are from Medicaid claims which reflect spending at the time health care services are used or premium payments are made, whereas other measures of spending may reflect the timing of state payments to providers. Additionally, estimates here do not include prescription drug rebates and most supplemental payments to providers, which other Medicaid spending sources may include.  A small number of Medicaid enrollees whose eligibility group is unknown are not broken out into a separate column but are included in state and national totals. States with "Unusable" data quality according to the CMS DQ Atlas indicators "Medicaid-Only Enrollment", "Eligibility Group Code", "Age", "Restricted Benefits Code" and "Medicaid Beneficiary Expenditures" are not included in these tables and are not included in national totals. For more information on data quality issues that may lead to unusual spending or enrollment, see CMS DQ Atlas. Caution is recommended when comparing data between years since different states may be included in each year due to data quality.

Definitions

*N/A:* Not Applicable. These states had not adopted the ACA Medicaid expansion as of the reporting year. *NSD:* Not Significant Data. Due to data security measures, the values of cells with fewer than 50 enrollees are not reported but are included in state and national totals. *DQ:* Data Quality. Data categorized as "Unusable" based on the DQ Atlas. These values are not included in the national totals, unless otherwise specified in a footnote. *Full-Benefit Medicaid Enrollees:* Full-benefit enrollees are those who are enrolled in Medicaid for at least 1 month with full benefits or those who received at least one month of benefits through an alternative package of benchmark equivalent coverage. They may have not actually used any services during this period, but they are reported as enrolled in the program and are eligible to receive services. *Seniors:* Enrollees age 65 and older. *People with Disabilities:* Enrollees under age 65 who are reported as eligible due to a disability. *Adults:* Enrollees ages 19 to 64 who are not eligible due to a disability or newly eligible for Medicaid by the ACA Medicaid expansion. *Children:* Enrollees ages 18 and younger who are not eligible due to a disability. *ACA Expansion Adults:* Enrollees ages 19 to 64 who were made newly eligible for Medicaid by the ACA Medicaid expansion.

Notes

Estimates here may not match other sources due to enrollment categorization differences and differences in measures of Medicaid spending. The spending data used in these estimates are from Medicaid claims which reflect spending at the time health care services are used or premium payments are made, whereas other measures of spending may reflect the timing of state payments to providers. Additionally, estimates here do not include prescription drug rebates and most supplemental payments to providers, which other Medicaid spending sources may include. A small number of Medicaid enrollees whose eligibility group is unknown are not broken out into a separate column but are included in state and national totals. States with "Unusable" data quality according to the CMS DQ Atlas indicators "Medicaid-Only Enrollment", "Eligibility Group Code", "Age" and "Medicaid Beneficiary Expenditures" are not included in these tables and are not included in national totals. For more information on data quality issues that may lead to unusual spending or enrollment, see CMS DQ Atlas. Caution is recommended when comparing data between years since different states may be included in each year due to data quality.
Sources
KFF analysis of the T-MSIS Research Identifiable Files, 2023 (Preliminary)
Definitions

*N/A:* Not Applicable. These states had not adopted the ACA Medicaid expansion as of the reporting year. *NSD:* Not Significant Data. Due to data security measures, the values of cells with fewer than 50 enrollees are not reported but are included in state and national totals. *DQ:* Data Quality. Data categorized as "Unusable" based on the DQ Atlas. These values are not included in the national totals, unless otherwise specified in a footnote. *Enrollees:* Individuals who are enrolled in Medicaid at any time during the calendar year. *Seniors:* Enrollees age 65 and older. *People with Disabilities:* Enrollees under age 65 who are reported as eligible due to a disability. *Adults:* Enrollees ages 19 to 64 who are not eligible due to a disability or newly eligible for Medicaid by the ACA Medicaid expansion. *Children:* Enrollees ages 18 and younger who are not eligible due to a disability. *ACA Expansion Adults:* Enrollees ages 19 to 64 who were made newly eligible for Medicaid by the ACA Medicaid expansion.

Notes

The amount of Federal payments to a State for medical services depends on two factors. The first is the actual amount spent that qualifies as matchable under Medicaid and the FMAP. The *Federal Medical Assistance Percentage (FMAP)* is computed from a formula that takes into account the average per capita income for each State relative to the national average. By law, the FMAP cannot be less than 50%. For more information, please see the KFF policy brief [Medicaid Financing: The Basics](https://www.kff.org/medicaid/issue-brief/medicaid-financing-the-basics/). The *multiplier* is based on the FMAP. For every dollar the state spends on Medicaid, the federal government matches at a rate that varies year to year. For FY 2004, the rate for Alabama was 1:2.80 (73.70%). For information about the multiplier effect of Medicaid spending, see The Kaiser Commission on Medicaid's policy brief released in December 2008, [Role of Medicaid in State Economies: A Look at the Research](https://www.kff.org/medicaid/the-role-of-medicaid-in-state-economies/).
Sources

KFF estimates of increased FY 2027 FMAPs based on [Federal Register, November 28, 2025 (Vol 90, No. 227), pp 54697-54698](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/FR-2025-11-28/pdf/2025-21332.pdf). KCMU estimates of the multiplier are based on the FMAP.

The Families First and Coronavirus Response Act (FFCRA) provided a temporary 6.2 percentage point increase to each state's federal medical assistance percentage (FMAP) during the COVID-19 public health emergency beginning in the second quarter of FY 2020 through the second quarter of FY 2023. This enhanced FMAP then phased down to 5.0 percentage points for the third quarter of FY 2023, and 2.5 percentage points for the fourth quarter of FY 2023.
The American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 (ARPA) increased DSH allotments to states during the COVID-19 public health emergency.
Under the Affordable Care Act (ACA), DSH allotments were slated to be reduced beginning in FY 2014. However, subsequent legislation delayed those reductions until FY 2025.

Definitions

*Disproportionate Share Hospital (DSH)* adjustment payments provide additional help to those hospitals that serve a significantly disproportionate number of low-income patients; eligible hospitals are referred to as DSH hospitals. States receive an annual DSH allotment to cover the costs of DSH hospitals that provide care to low-income patients that are not paid by other payers, such as Medicare, Medicaid, the Children’s Health Insurance Program (CHIP) or other health insurance. This annual allotment is calculated by law and includes requirements to ensure that the DSH payments to individual DSH hospitals are not higher than these actual uncompensated costs. *Federal Fiscal Year*: Unless otherwise noted, years preceded by "FY" on [State Health Facts](https://www.kff.org/statedata/) refer to the Federal Fiscal Year, which runs from October 1 through September 30. For example, FY 2018 refers to the period from October 1, 2017 through September 30, 2018. *N/A*: Not applicable.

Total SSDI Beneficiaries and SSI Beneficiaries, Under Age 65
Footnotes
United States: An additional 38,357 children under age 18 and students aged 18–19 received SSI and SSDI in the U.S.
Notes

SSDI and SSI beneficiaries are reported as of December in the year specified. SSDI beneficiaries include disabled workers, widowers, and adult children. To be eligible for the Social Security Disability Insurance Program, a person must be insured for benefits, be younger than full retirement age, have filed an application for benefits, and have a Social Security-defined disability. To be eligible for the Supplemental Security Income Program, a person must be age 65 or older, blind, or disabled, and have limited income, limited resources, have filed an application for benefits, and met certain other requirements. The total number of SSDI and SSI beneficiaries is calculated as a sum of the state data and excludes United States territories and foreign countries.    Population numbers are rounded to the nearest 100.
Sources

Social Security Administration, Annual Statistical Report on the Social Security Disability Insurance Program, 2022 (Table 9). Social Security Administration, Annual Statistical Report on the Supplemental Security Income Program, 2022 (Tables 10 and 16).
Definitions

*Social Security Disability Insurance (SSDI)* is a federal entitlement program that provides disability payments to individuals (and in certain instances to their surviving spouses and children) who have been (a) deemed medically disabled due to a physical or mental impairment that prevents them from working for a year or more or that is expected to result in death; and who (b) have earned enough work credits to receive SSDI payments (the number of credits needed depends on age). Federal law requires a 5-month waiting period after becoming disabled before receipt of SSDI cash benefits. SSDI recipients are eligible for Medicare coverage following an additional 24-month waiting period, resulting in a total of 29 months before receipt of health benefits through Medicare for SSDI recipients.    *Supplemental Security Income (SSI)* is a federal entitlement program that provides cash assistance to low-income aged, blind, and disabled individuals. Individuals receiving SSI benefits are eligible for Medicaid coverage in all states except "section 209(b)" states, which have opted to use their more restrictive 1972 criteria in determining Medicaid eligibility for SSI recipients. Section 209(b) of the 1972 amendments to the Social Security Act allowed states the option of continuing to use their own eligibility criteria in determining Medicaid eligibility for the elderly and disabled rather than extending Medicaid coverage to all of those individuals who qualify for SSI benefits. As of 2001, eleven states (Connecticut, Hawaii, Illinois, Indiana, Minnesota, Missouri, New Hampshire, North Dakota, Ohio, Oklahoma, and Virginia) had elected the "209(b)" option to apply their 1972 eligibility criteria to aged or disabled individuals receiving SSI benefits for purposes of determining Medicaid eligibility.   *Disabled widow(er)*: must be disabled and be at least 50 years old.   *Disabled worker*: beneficiary who worked in employment covered by Social Security long enough to be insured and who had been working recently in employment covered by Social Security prior to disability onset.   *Adult children*: adult disabled before the age of 22.

Definitions

*Social Security Disability Insurance (SSDI)* is a federal entitlement program that provides disability payments to individuals (and in certain instances to their surviving spouses and children) who have been (a) deemed medically disabled due to a physical or mental impairment that prevents them from working for a year or more or that is expected to result in death; and who (b) have earned enough work credits to receive SSDI payments (the number of credits needed depends on age). Federal law requires a 5-month waiting period after becoming disabled before receipt of SSDI cash benefits. SSDI recipients are eligible for Medicare coverage following an additional 24-month waiting period, resulting in a total of 29 months before receipt of health benefits through Medicare for SSDI recipients.   *Supplemental Security Income (SSI)* is a federal entitlement program that provides cash assistance to low-income aged, blind, and disabled individuals. Individuals receiving SSI benefits are eligible for Medicaid coverage in all states except "section 209(b)" states, which have opted to use their more restrictive 1972 criteria in determining Medicaid eligibility for SSI recipients. Section 209(b) of the 1972 amendments to the Social Security Act allowed states the option of continuing to use their own eligibility criteria in determining Medicaid eligibility for the elderly and disabled rather than extending Medicaid coverage to all of those individuals who qualify for SSI benefits. As of 2001, eleven states (Connecticut, Hawaii, Illinois, Indiana, Minnesota, Missouri, New Hampshire, North Dakota, Ohio, Oklahoma, and Virginia) had elected the "209(b)" option to apply their 1972 eligibility criteria to aged or disabled individuals receiving SSI benefits for purposes of determining Medicaid eligibility.   *Disabled worker*: beneficiary who worked in employment covered by Social Security long enough to be insured and who had been working recently in employment covered by Social Security prior to disability onset.   *Disabled widow(er)*: must be disabled and be at least 50 years old.   *Adult children*: adult disabled before the age of 22.   *Aged*: a person who is age 65 or older.   *Blindness*: a condition in which a person has a central visual acuity for distance of 20/200 or less in their better eye with use of a correcting lens; or they have a visual field limitation in their better eye, such that the widest diameter of the visual field subtends an angle no greater than 20 degrees.   *Disabled child*: a person under age 18 who has a medically determinable physical or mental impairment, (including an emotional or learning problem) that: results marked and severe functional limitations; and can be expected to result in death; or has lasted or can be expected to last for a continuous period of not less than 12 months.   *Disabled adult*: a person who is age 18 or older who has a medically determinable physical or mental impairment, (including an emotional or learning problem) that: results in the inability to do any substantial gainful activity; and can be expected to result in death; or has lasted or can be expected to last for a continuous period of not less than 12 months.
